title: AI is reshaping hiring before it reshapes jobs
full_text:
IN PARTNERSHIP WITH Deloitte Over the past year, businesses at home and abroad have announced restructures, redundancies and hiring changes linked to automation and AI adoption, fuelling concerns that widespread workforce disruption is already under way.
Many of these announcements have been quite prominent, but they are not necessarily a good barometer for overall labour market performance.
As Deloitte Access Economics’ latest Employment Forecasts report shows, the broader labour market impacts of AI are only just starting to become visible – but those impacts appear to be felt more in recruitment than redundancies and are not occurring evenly across industries or organisations.
Our latest analysis identifies 82 occupations that are particularly exposed to AI disruption, according to Deloitte Human Capital’s Work Analyser tool. These occupations are assessed on the extent to which their core tasks can be automated by AI versus the necessity for human performance.
Many are concentrated in white-collar industries such as financial services, professional services and information media. Some of the occupations most exposed include systems analysts, project administrators, payroll clerks and a range of clerical roles.
What our analysis finds is that AI exposure does not automatically translate into job losses. The more obvious signs of disruption appear in hiring activity. Job vacancies in several AI-disrupted occupations have weakened, suggesting employers may be becoming more selective about hiring as they assess AI’s role in their business.
What does this mean going forward? Historically, technological change often affects hiring before it affects headcount. Organisations slow recruitment and reorganise workflows while existing employees remain in place. Current labour market data suggests AI may be following a similar path.
Against a backdrop of a slowing labour market, we expect growth in AI-disrupted occupations to weaken over the next five years but remain positive. Our modelling suggests annual employment growth will slow from an average of 1.9 per cent over the past five years to 1.2 per cent under a baseline scenario without AI.
Once the effects of AI adoption are incorporated, that growth rate may fall further to just 0.5 per cent a year for the set of AI-disrupted occupations.
By contrast, occupations expected to be enhanced by AI tell a very different story. These are roles that rely heavily on judgment, creativity, leadership and interpersonal skills – capabilities that AI can enhance but not easily replicate.
While employment growth in these occupations would otherwise slow to around 0.9 per cent annually, AI adoption is forecast to lift their growth to 2.2 per cent a year over the next five years.
This divergence is important because it challenges one of the most common assumptions in the public debate: The question of whether AI will create or destroy jobs. The evidence suggests it is more likely to reshape demand across occupations, slowing growth in some parts of the labour market over the next few years while increasing demand in others.
Beyond that horizon, the labour market impacts of AI will depend less on the technology itself and more on how organisations redesign work to integrate it at scale. Current evidence suggests AI is being used primarily to augment human capability rather than replace it.
As adoption matures, the focus will shift from task automation to work redesign and workforce transition, changing the skills, roles and capabilities organisations need to create value. Those that most effectively combine human judgment and AI, and reshape their workforces in lockstep with technology adoption, are likely to realise the greatest productivity gains.
The result is unlikely to be uniform job loss or growth, but a reshaping of demand across occupations, skills and industries. This phase could have much larger implications for workforce demand and skills requirements but may also be fundamentally constrained by the economic reality of AI.
The true cost of AI usage – measured by tokens consumed – is increasingly being reflected in AI pricing models, making it fundamentally uneconomic for organisations to deploy AI everywhere, all the time. Already we are hearing reports of large organisations limiting AI usage to keep costs down.
This means that going forward, the organisations that gain the most value from AI are unlikely to be those that automate the largest number of tasks. They will be the organisations that identify where human judgment remains essential, where AI can augment capability, and how work should be redesigned to combine the strengths of both.
The impact of this dynamic on the broader labour market is hard to predict, but it will almost certainly be uneven, changing demand for certain roles and skills and creating winners and losers in the process.
The best move is to avoid sitting still: executives should not be complacent in their existing business models and workforce strategies, and workers should seek to upskill and retrain in AI.
David Rumbens is Partner at Deloitte Access Economics. Sarah Rogers is Lead Partner, Workforce Strategy and Design, Deloitte Australia.
.
